canonical_summary:
Drafting an ITU coordination filing for a satellite network is a structured exercise in matching a regulatory clock to a technical dataset. The international rules sit in the ITU Radio Regulations: Article 9 governs advance publication and coordination, Article 11 governs notification and recording in the Master International Frequency Register, and Appendix 4 prescribes the exact characteristics that must be supplied at each stage. Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) layers due diligence obligations on top, and No. 11.44 imposes the seven-year deadline from receipt of the API or CR/C to bringing into use.

A correct filing begins with the Advance Publication Information (API) under No. 9.1, proceeds to a Coordination Request (CR/C) under Section II of Article 9 for networks subject to No. 9.7, 9.7A, 9.7B, 9.11 to 9.14, or 9.21, and ends with an Article 11 notification submitted no earlier than three years before bringing into use. Each step uses Annex 2A, 2B, or 2C of Appendix 4 and must pass SpaceCap and SpaceVal validation before the Bureau of Radiocommunication (BR) will publish it in the BR International Frequency Information Circular (BR IFIC).

Only an ITU Member State administration can file. The operator must therefore secure a notifying administration, typically the regulator with national nexus (FCC in the United States, Ofcom in the United Kingdom, ANFR in France, BNetzA in Germany, WPC and IN-SPACe in India), and bear the Council Decision 482 cost-recovery fees. The drafting work is the operator's; the legal submission is the administration's.
h1: How to draft an ITU coordination filing: Article 9, Appendix 4, and the seven-year clock
meta_title: How to draft an ITU coordination filing
meta_description: Step by step guide to drafting an ITU satellite coordination filing under Article 9, Article 11, Appendix 4, Resolution 49, and the No. 11.44 BIU deadline.

sections.heading: What the ITU is and why coordination matters
paragraphs: The International Telecommunication Union (ITU) is the United Nations specialized agency for information and communication technologies. Its Radiocommunication Sector (ITU-R), through the Radiocommunication Bureau (BR), administers the Radio Regulations (RR), the binding treaty text that allocates the radio frequency spectrum and the geostationary and non-geostationary satellite orbits among Member States.

A satellite network can only claim international recognition and interference protection by completing the procedures in the Radio Regulations. The current consolidated text in force is the Edition of 2020, with amendments adopted at WRC-19 and WRC-23. Without a recorded assignment in the Master International Frequency Register (MIFR), a network has no priority date and no entitlement to protection from later filings.

Coordination matters because spectrum and orbit are finite and shared. The BR uses the priority date of a complete filing to determine which administration must coordinate with which, and the four month reaction window under No. 9.52 starts the bilateral negotiation that ultimately decides whether a network can operate without causing or claiming protection from harmful interference.
bullets: Treaty source: ITU Radio Regulations, Edition of 2020.
Procedural core: Article 9 (coordination), Article 11 (notification and recording), Appendix 4 (data requirements).
Due diligence overlay: Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19).
Enforcement: cancellation under No. 11.44 or No. 13.6 inquiry.

sections.heading: The Article 9 and Article 11 framework
paragraphs: Article 9 is split into two sections. Section I (Nos. 9.1 to 9.2B) covers Advance Publication Information (API). Section II (Nos. 9.6 to 9.21) covers the Coordination Request (CR/C) and identifies the specific provisions that trigger coordination: No. 9.7 for geostationary-to-geostationary networks, No. 9.7A and 9.7B for interactions between GSO networks and earth stations of non-GSO systems, Nos. 9.11 to 9.14 for various inter-service and terrestrial triggers, and No. 9.21 for seeking agreement of other administrations.

Article 11 handles notification and recording. No. 11.2 obliges an administration to notify any assignment capable of causing harmful interference to another administration or used for international radiocommunication. No. 11.15 sets the earliest notification date at three years before bringing into use. No. 11.27 requires the BR to examine notices for conformity with the Table of Frequency Allocations, No. 11.31 checks the allocation itself, and Nos. 11.32 and 11.32A check the probability of harmful interference and conformity with Article 9 coordination. No. 11.41 allows provisional recording when coordination is incomplete but the assignment has been put into service.

Bringing into use is defined in No. 11.44 and No. 11.44B. The seven-year regulatory deadline runs from the date the BR receives the relevant complete information under No. 9.1, 9.1A, or 9.2. If the deadline is missed, the filing is cancelled.

subsections.heading: Section I of Article 9: Advance Publication
paragraphs: Under No. 9.1, the administration sends advance publication information to the BR not earlier than seven years and preferably not later than two years before the planned bringing-into-use date. The BR publishes the data in an API/A special section of the BR IFIC within three months. Following WRC-15 (Resolution 906), the API is effectively merged with the CR/C for networks subject to Section II coordination, so a stand-alone API is now required only for networks that are not subject to coordination (for example, some non-GSO systems falling outside specific Section II triggers, or networks subject only to inter-administration agreement under No. 9.21).

subsections.heading: Section II of Article 9: Coordination
paragraphs: The CR/C carries the full Appendix 4 Annex 2B dataset. The BR examines the submission under No. 9.34, identifies affected administrations whose networks may be interfered with, and publishes the CR/C in the BR IFIC. From the date of publication, affected administrations have four months under No. 9.52 to react. Failure to react means the network is considered to have no objection, with the consequences described in Nos. 9.48 and 9.49.

sections.heading: API filing: what goes in
paragraphs: The API stage uses Annex 2A of Appendix 4. The notifying administration supplies a general description of the planned satellite network or system, including the orbital parameters, the frequency bands, the polarization, the service area, the antenna patterns, the maximum equivalent isotropic radiated power (EIRP), the power flux density at the Earth's surface, the receiver characteristics, and, for non-GSO systems, the orbital parameters per plane (inclination, number of satellites, altitude or period, and reference body).

The data must be entered in the BR's SpaceCap software and validated with SpaceVal. Antenna gain contours and service area polygons are submitted in GIMS format under Resolution 55. Fatal errors prevent forwarding by the administration. The BR's three-month publication target only starts once a clean dataset is received.
bullets: Window: not earlier than seven years and preferably not later than two years before BIU.
Data set: Appendix 4 Annex 2A characteristics.
Software: SpaceCap for the data, GIMS for graphics, SpaceVal for validation.
Output: API/A special section in the BR IFIC, typically within three months of receipt.
For coordinated networks: the API is effectively merged with the CR/C after WRC-15 streamlining.

sections.heading: CR/C filing and the coordination phase
paragraphs: The Coordination Request is the engine of the international process. The notifying administration files a complete Appendix 4 Annex 2B dataset, more detailed than the API: every assigned frequency, every antenna characteristic, transmitter power and emission designators, modulation, link budgets, polarization, service area, and the full earth station characteristics for the worst-case station that defines the coordination contour.

The BR examines the submission under No. 9.34 and No. 9.36, identifies affected administrations under the criteria of Appendix 5 (for example, the C/I or DeltaT/T thresholds for No. 9.7), and publishes the CR/C as a special section in the BR IFIC. Typical BR publication lag is around four months. From the publication date, affected administrations have four months under No. 9.52 to send comments or agreements.

Bilateral coordination then begins and has no fixed statutory length. GSO-to-GSO coordination commonly runs years, and contested orbital slots have taken more than a decade. The operator's drafting choices in the CR/C, particularly the declared coverage area, peak EIRP, and antenna pointing flexibility, directly determine which administrations are pulled in under No. 9.36 and therefore the size and shape of the negotiation that follows.

subsections.heading: Common No. 9 triggers in commercial bands
bullets: No. 9.7: GSO-to-GSO coordination, arc-based identification.
No. 9.7A and 9.7B: GSO networks vs. non-GSO earth stations and vice versa.
No. 9.11: BSS in the band 620-790 MHz vs. terrestrial.
No. 9.11A: space-to-Earth, Earth-to-space, and space-to-space coordination.
No. 9.12, 9.12A, 9.13: non-GSO vs. non-GSO, non-GSO vs. GSO MSS feeder links.
No. 9.14: space stations vs. terrestrial stations.
No. 9.21: agreement of other administrations under specific footnotes.

subsections.heading: What well-drafted Appendix 4 Annex 2B data looks like
bullets: Realistic, not aspirational, EIRP and PFD values: inflated numbers expand the No. 9.36 affected-administration set unnecessarily.
Antenna gain contours that match the actual spacecraft, supplied in GIMS.
Earth station characteristics that bound the typical and worst-case terminal.
Orbital parameters consistent with the licence application filed nationally.
Service area polygons drawn deliberately, not as a global default.

sections.heading: The BIU rule and the seven-year deadline
paragraphs: No. 11.44 sets the hard deadline: any frequency assignment to a space station of a satellite network must be brought into use within seven years following the date of receipt by the BR of the relevant complete information under No. 9.1, 9.1A, or 9.2. The BR cancels assignments that miss this date after a three-month warning under No. 11.44.

No. 11.44B defines bringing into use for the geostationary orbit. A space station capable of transmitting or receiving the notified frequency assignment must be deployed at the notified orbital position and maintained there for a continuous period of 90 days. The notifying administration must inform the BR within 30 days from the end of the 90-day period. The notified BIU date is the first day of the 90-day period, not the last. No. 11.44C, 11.44D, and 11.44E set parallel 90-day rules for non-GSO networks.

Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) requires the administration to file due diligence information identifying the satellite name, the spacecraft manufacturer and contract date, the launch services provider, the launch vehicle, the launch site, and the predicted BIU date. Missing this information by the regulatory deadline triggers cancellation under No. 13.6 procedures.

Suspension under No. 11.49 is permitted for up to three years (extendable in defined cases such as launch failure). Bringing back into use after suspension requires a fresh 90-day demonstration. Resolution 40 (WRC-15) permits the same satellite to drift between slots to bring multiple networks into use successively, but with reporting obligations on the previous orbital position, the arrival and departure dates, and any prior BIU use of the same spacecraft.
bullets: Seven-year clock starts at BR receipt of complete information under No. 9.1, 9.1A, or 9.2.
GSO BIU: 90 continuous days at the notified orbital position, capability on the notified frequency, 30 days to report.
Non-GSO BIU: 90 days under No. 11.44C through 11.44E plus Resolution 35 (Rev. WRC-23) milestones (10% by year 2 after the 7-year date, 50% by year 5, 100% by year 7).
Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) due diligence is a separate cancellation trigger.
Resolution 40 (WRC-15) allows successive BIU but requires disclosure.

sections.heading: Choosing a notifying administration
paragraphs: Only an ITU Member State administration may file. The operator chooses the administration that will sponsor the filing, which need not be the operator's home jurisdiction, but every administration imposes a national nexus and procedural overlay before it will forward a submission to the BR.

The United States (FCC) requires every Part 25 space station applicant to submit Appendix 4 data with the licence application, validate it with BR software, and clear all fatal errors. The applicant is unconditionally liable for ITU cost-recovery fees under Council Decision 482, and notification must be filed within seven years (eight for planned bands).

The United Kingdom (Ofcom) sponsors filings for organisations registered in the UK, the British Overseas Territories, the Channel Islands, and the Isle of Man, under the Procedures for the Management of Satellite Filings. Ofcom requires demonstration that reasonable coordination effort has been made before notification.

France (ANFR) files for ESA, EUTELSAT IGO, the Galileo programme, French government operators, and commercial operators with a French establishment. Germany (BNetzA) applies the VVSatSys procedure: frequency allocation verification, financial capacity for ITU fees, qualified staff for coordination meetings, and compatibility studies with German networks. India (WPC, under DoT) requires an IN-SPACe advisory note before the WPC technical evaluation, and a +/- 5 degree interference analysis for GSO requests.

subsections.heading: Selection criteria operators actually weigh
bullets: National nexus: licence, registration, or establishment in the jurisdiction.
Filing date priority: how quickly the administration will forward a clean submission.
Cost-recovery exposure under Council Decision 482 and any national fees.
Coordination support: whether the administration leads or expects the operator to drive bilaterals.

Existing filing portfolio: piggybacking on the administration's prior priority dates where possible (Starlink uses both the United States and Germany; OneWeb uses the United Kingdom with French support).

sections.heading: Practical advice on drafting the filing
paragraphs: Treat the filing as a single document with three temporal layers. The API or merged API/CR/C captures the network on the day of receipt; the CR/C defines the coordination footprint that drives bilaterals; the Article 11 notification crystallizes the recorded assignment. The Appendix 4 dataset must be internally consistent across all three.

Start the SpaceCap entry as soon as the spacecraft design is stable enough that the antenna pattern, EIRP, and frequency plan will not move materially. Filing too early forces refilings and can lose priority; filing too late compresses the seven-year BIU window. The practical sweet spot is around six years before the planned launch, leaving twelve months for coordination warm-up.

Be conservative on declared parameters that drive No. 9.36 identification. An over-stated EIRP or coverage polygon pulls in administrations that have no real interference relationship with the network, multiplying coordination work without strategic benefit. Be liberal on parameters that protect operational flexibility, such as the range of supported emission designators and the assigned frequency blocks within the licensed band.

Plan the BIU evidence from day one of the filing. Identify the spacecraft that will sit at the notified longitude for 90 continuous days, the launch slot, and the in-orbit testing plan. Maintain the Resolution 49 due diligence dataset live; update it whenever the manufacturer, launch provider, or launch site changes. Document continuous occupancy with telemetry suitable for a No. 13.6 inquiry.
bullets: Draft once, file three times: API, CR/C, Article 11 notification all derive from the same master dataset.
Validate with SpaceCap and SpaceVal before sending to the administration.
Use GIMS for antenna contours and service area, in line with Resolution 55.
Calendar the seven-year deadline from BR receipt, not from the date you filed with the administration.
Treat Resolution 49 due diligence as a live document, not a one-time form.
Keep BIU telemetry from the first 90 days available for the BR and for any 13.6 inquiry.

faqs.question: What is the difference between API and CR/C in an ITU coordination filing?
faqs.answer: The Advance Publication Information (API) under No. 9.1 of Article 9 is a general description of a planned satellite network filed not earlier than seven years before bringing into use, using Annex 2A of Appendix 4. The Coordination Request (CR/C) under Section II of Article 9 is the substantive coordination submission, using the more detailed Annex 2B dataset, that triggers the four-month reaction window of No. 9.52 for affected administrations. After WRC-15 streamlining, for networks subject to coordination under No. 9.7, 9.7A, 9.7B, 9.11 to 9.14, or 9.21, the API is effectively merged with the CR/C.

faqs.question: When does the seven-year ITU bringing-into-use deadline start?
faqs.answer: Under No. 11.44 of Article 11, the seven-year deadline starts on the date the Bureau of Radiocommunication (BR) receives the relevant complete information under No. 9.1, 9.1A, or 9.2. It is not the date the administration forwarded the filing, and it is not the date of BR IFIC publication. If the data is incomplete, the clock only starts when a clean dataset is received. The BR warns the administration three months before expiry under No. 11.44, and if bringing into use is not achieved in time the assignment is cancelled.

faqs.question: What exactly counts as bringing into use under No. 11.44B?
faqs.answer: For a geostationary network, No. 11.44B requires a space station capable of transmitting or receiving on the notified frequency assignment to be deployed at the notified orbital position and maintained there for a continuous period of 90 days. The notifying administration must inform the BR within 30 days after the 90-day period ends, and the notified BIU date is the first day of the 90 days. Capability, location, and continuity are all required. Footnote 11.44B.2 extends the continuity requirement beyond 90 days when the notification arrives more than 120 days after the claimed BIU date.

faqs.question: Can the same satellite bring multiple ITU filings into use?
faqs.answer: Yes, but with disclosure. Resolution 40 (WRC-15) permits a single space station to be moved from one notified orbital position to another to bring multiple geostationary networks into use successively. Each BIU must independently satisfy No. 11.44B: 90 continuous days at the notified longitude, capability on the notified frequency. The notifying administration must report the previous orbital position, the arrival and departure dates, and any prior use of the same spacecraft for BIU. Resolution 40 applies to both initial BIU under 11.44B and to bringing back into use after suspension under No. 11.49.

faqs.question: What data does Appendix 4 require for a coordination filing?
faqs.answer: Appendix 4 prescribes the consolidated characteristics list. Annex 2A applies to API, Annex 2B to coordination under Article 9, and Annex 2C to notification under Article 11. The data set includes orbital parameters (longitude for GSO, plane characteristics for non-GSO), frequency bands and assigned frequencies, antenna gain contours and pointing, transmitter power and emission designators, polarization, service area, modulation, link budgets, and earth station characteristics. The BR uses SpaceCap and SpaceVal to validate the dataset, and graphical data such as gain contours and service area polygons must be submitted in GIMS format per Resolution 55.

faqs.question: Who can submit an ITU satellite filing?
faqs.answer: Only an ITU Member State administration, acting as the notifying administration, can submit Advance Publication Information, Coordination Requests, or Notifications to the BR. A satellite operator cannot file directly. The operator prepares the Appendix 4 dataset and submits it to its chosen national regulator through the e-Submission interface. The regulator validates the package, applies its own national pre-conditions, and forwards it to the BR. The choice of administration does not need to be the operator's home country, but in practice every administration requires some form of national nexus, such as a licence, registration, or establishment.

faqs.question: What does Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) require?
faqs.answer: Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-19) imposes administrative due diligence. Within the bringing-into-use window the notifying administration must file information identifying the satellite name, the spacecraft manufacturer, the contract date, the launch services provider, the launch vehicle, the launch site, and the predicted BIU date. Failure to provide this information by the regulatory deadline causes the BR to cancel the filing under the No. 13.6 procedures. Resolution 49 is a separate cancellation trigger from the No. 11.44 seven-year deadline, and operators should treat it as a live document that is updated whenever any of the underlying facts change.

faqs.question: How long does ITU coordination actually take?
faqs.answer: There is no fixed statutory duration for bilateral coordination. The BR typically publishes the CR/C special section about four months after receipt of a clean submission. Affected administrations identified under No. 9.36 then have four months from publication to react under No. 9.52. After that, bilateral negotiation begins and routinely runs several years for GSO-to-GSO cases; contested orbital slots have taken more than a decade. The end-to-end path from API to Article 11 notification commonly takes five to seven years and frequently consumes the full seven-year BIU allowance set by No. 11.44.

faqs.question: What happens if I miss the seven-year BIU deadline?
faqs.answer: The BR cancels the frequency assignment under No. 11.44. Three months before the expiry of the seven-year period, the BR notifies the administration. If BIU is not confirmed in time, the entry is removed and the priority date is lost. A limited suspension is available under No. 11.49 for up to three years, extendable in defined cases such as a launch failure, but bringing back into use after suspension requires a fresh 90-day demonstration under No. 11.49.1 for GSO networks. Resolution 49 due diligence failures are an independent cancellation route under No. 13.6 procedures.

faqs.question: Do non-GSO constellations follow the same BIU rules?
faqs.answer: The 90-day mechanic is the same. Nos. 11.44C, 11.44D, and 11.44E set parallel 90-day rules for non-GSO networks in the fixed-satellite, mobile-satellite, and broadcasting-satellite services, requiring at least one space station to be deployed and capable on the notified frequency assignment. On top of that, Resolution 35 (Rev. WRC-23) imposes a milestone-based deployment regime for non-GSO constellations: 10% of the notified number of satellites within two years after the seven-year BIU date, 50% within five years, and 100% within seven years. Missing a milestone reduces the recorded number of satellites to those actually deployed.

faqs.question: What does an FCC ITU filing require in the United States?
faqs.answer: The FCC requires every Part 25 space station applicant to submit Appendix 4 data with its licence application, populate the ITU database using BR software (SpaceCap, SpaceVal, GIMS), and clear all fatal errors before the FCC will forward the filing to the BR. The applicant or licensee is unconditionally responsible for paying ITU cost-recovery fees directly under Council Decision 482; non-payment causes cancellation. Notification must be submitted within seven years of the initial filing, or eight years for planned bands. The FCC publishes detailed guidance under How to Prepare a USA ITU filing and the FAQ on Processing of Space Station Applications.

faqs.question: Which administrations file for Starlink, OneWeb, Kuiper, and Telesat?
faqs.answer: SpaceX Starlink is notified primarily by the United States through the FCC and NTIA (filings such as STEAM-1 and STEAM-2), with parallel Ku and Ka filings made by Germany. On 15 October 2019 the United States submitted ITU filings for 30,000 additional Gen2 Starlink satellites on top of the 12,000 already covered. OneWeb is notified by the United Kingdom (Ofcom) under filings L5 and L5-A, with supplementary filings via France for some bands. Amazon Kuiper is notified by the United States. Telesat Lightspeed is notified by Canada through ISED.
